items.title: State the case you're analyzing
items.avoid: Saying 'O(1)' without saying whether it's best / average / amortized / worst.
items.prefer: Be explicit: 'amortized O(1) per insert; worst case O(n) due to occasional resize'.
items.why: Wrong-case assumptions cause production surprises (e.g. one O(n) operation in a hot loop).
items.example:
```python
# `list.append` is amortized O(1); under load, occasional resizes are O(n).
```

items.title: Use the doubling test to confirm complexity
items.prefer: Run the algorithm at n, 2n, 4n, 8n and observe the runtime ratio.
items.why: Linear scaling doubles time; quadratic quadruples; logarithmic barely changes — a fast empirical sanity check.
items.example:
```python
import time
for n in (10_000, 20_000, 40_000):
    t = time.perf_counter()
    f(generate(n))
    print(n, time.perf_counter() - t)
```

items.title: Pick the data structure that matches the access pattern
items.avoid: Defaulting to a list (or any one structure) for every workload.
items.prefer: Use Big-O of operations to pick: dict for keyed access (O(1)), heap for top-k (O(log n)), sorted structure for ranges (O(log n)).
items.why: Structure choice changes whole-program complexity, often dropping it by one or two classes.
items.example:
```python
seen = set()        # O(1) membership instead of O(n) list-in
for x in xs:
    if x in seen: continue
    seen.add(x)
```

items.title: Profile before micro-optimizing
items.avoid: Hand-tuning a function that's already negligible.
items.prefer: Find the asymptotic hotspot first; a 100× speedup of an O(n) routine doesn't help if an O(n²) routine elsewhere dominates.
items.why: Engineering effort follows asymptotic cost, not gut feeling.

items.title: Track space as well as time
items.avoid: Optimizing time at the cost of memory you don't have.
items.prefer: Quote both: 'O(n) time, O(1) extra space' or 'O(n) time, O(n) memo table'.
items.why: An algorithm that doesn't fit in RAM isn't faster than the slow one that does.
items.example:
```python
# Streaming top-k uses O(k) space; full sort uses O(n).
import heapq
top = heapq.nlargest(10, scores)   # O(k) extra memory
```
